original text:
Work with Bill Richard in Savannah, GA to get a business insurance quote and choose what products are right for you.
Bill Richard in Savannah, GA offers a State Farm® Personal Articles Policy. Add this to your property insurance to help cover your personal property against theft, accidental damage, or loss.
Ready to get started? Bill Richard in Savannah, GA can help you learn about State Farm Bank® and mobile banking.
State Farm® offers various types of affordable family health insurance. Bill Richard in Savannah, GA can help you choose the health insurance plan that's best for you.
Talk to Bill Richard in Savannah, GA to get annuity information and find the fund that meets your needs.
Contact Agent Bill Richard in Savannah, GA for investment information.
There's a lot to learn when you're getting started with life insurance. When you're ready, Bill Richard in Savannah, GA is here to help.
Shopping for car insurance? Bill Richard in Savannah, GA is one of more than 18,000 State Farm® agents nationwide who can help keep you and your family covered with quality auto insurance. You can talk to an agent at any time.
Make the move with Bill Richard in Savannah, GA to protect your home and family. Plus, save money when you have both car and home insurance with us.
There's a lot to learn when you're getting started with life insurance. When you're ready, Bill Richard in Savannah, GA is here to help.

original text:
Snooping in medicine cabinets used to be a lot more informing. (Uh, not that we've snooped.) Anyways, our point: A rising number of medicines are being used for reasons other than what it says on the label. Unlike prescription drug abuse of the past, this isn't all about the latest party drugs. Today, people are lured toward medicines for unique psychological benefits - whether to perk up a dud personality or calm jittery nerves. While pill-poppers have gotten loopy on codeine-based medications for years, now it's common for people to pop heart medicine to cure test anxiety or load up on narcolepsy meds to crank out a presentation.
Even the Food and Drug Administration can't keep up with drug companies hawking the marketable side effects of their remedies. A new report from the Kaiser Institute shows it takes the FDA 7 months to issue a warning for this type of illegal marketing, and companies average 4 months to take action. As a result, more people are popping pills for ailments for which drugs weren't tested and approved to treat - and patient safety is diminished. Before you get caught in a web of mixed medicines, consult this review of what's showing up in medicine cabinets, what they can do to you, and alternatives that are side-effect free.
What does it do? Creates a euphoric sense similar to cocaine. Improves mood, fatigue, sleepiness and cognition like caffeine, but for a longer period of time. Helps promote wakefulness and alertness.
Who gets prescribed this? Patients with Attention Deficit Disorder (ADD), Attention Deficit Hyperactive Disorder (ADHD), and narcolepsy. Provigil can also help with obstructive sleep apnea and is prescribed to elderly patients who need help waking up in the morning.
Who abuses this? Stimulants are the performance-enhancing drugs of the corporate world. Wall Street professionals and lawyers are said to like Provigil because of its easy accessibility, adding to it's appeal - this drug is less regulated than Ritalin and Adderall. Late-night partiers may also rely on these to get through the next day, says Dr. Tucker Woods, Chairman of the Department of Emergency Medicine at Long Island College Hospital of Brooklyn.
Addictive? Stimulants can be habit forming if patients rely on them for energy.
Alternative medicine: Because doctors recommend trying to minimize medications at all cost, stick with coffee. It tastes better, and your co-workers can't complain about you juicing. Other alternatives include natural stimulants like Ginseng, available at most health stores, although its effectiveness depends on the amount of extract, the type of ginseng, and the company that makes it. The Panax (also called Asian or Chinese) variety seems to work the best.
What does it do? Used to calm nerves, prevent anxiety attacks, and as a sleep aid.
Who gets prescribed this? Insomniacs and patients with anxiety problems like obsessive compulsive disorder (OCD) or social phobias. It can also be used to relax the muscles in patients with cerebral palsy or multiple sclerosis.
Who abuses this? These drugs create a calming effect, and recreate the effects of feeling drunk. Those who abuse this will lapse further into a more relaxed state. Desired by thrill-seekers wanting to intensify a drunken night out, these types of drugs have an additive effect when combined with alcohol, amplifying the effects and possibly causing death. "Alcohol slows down your breathing and benzodiazepines slow down your breathing in a different way - the combine effect can kill you," says Philip Wenger, PharmD, assistant professor at St. Louis College of Pharmacy.
Addictive? Yes, when used chronically.
Alternative medicine: If stress is what's eating you, try to make a habit of mid-afternoon workouts. A study by researchers at the University of Missouri-Columbia found high-intensity exercise greatly reduces stress and anxiety. Making sleep a routine can also help: try to get to bed at the same time and wake up at the same time every night. The herbal supplement, melatonin, is sometimes used for sleep and research shows it can help you nod off - especially if you're suffering from jet-lag.
What does it do? Antidepressants stimulate chemical changes that increase the levels of neurotransmitters associated with mood, including serotonin, norepinephrine and dopamine.
Who abuses this? Some use antidepressants as a quick-fix for moodiness, which critics say would be better addressed with a healthy diet and getting a full 8 hours of sleep.
Alternative medicine: Talk to a therapist and see if your depression is something you can work out through discussion, rather than meds. For over the counter stuff, St. John's Wort is a supplement sometimes taken for depression, but be careful because it can interact with post-surgery medicine and some high blood pressure prescriptions  - so check with your doctor first. Or you can eat your way to a good mood with grilled fish, like salmon. A study in Finland found that people who eat more fish are 31 percent less likely to suffer from depression because they contain feel-good omega-3 fatty acids.
What does it do? These sedatives relax the body by binding to neurotransmitters in the brain, producing a calming effect.
stimulants such as amphetamines (including methamphetamine), cocaine, and ecstasy. Some people take it also to get a high - they will fight the sleeping affects and try to stay awake because of the sensation it gives them, which include vivid visuals and a body high.
Addictive? This drug only takes about 15 minutes to kick in, so it can be habit-forming. Negative side affects can include hallucinations and forgetfulness.
Alternative medicine: Try a natural sleep aid, like melatonin, or an herbal supplement like Valerian. Or try some yoga poses before bed to stretch the body and relax the mind.
What does it do? This type of heart medication is used to treat high blood pressure and migraines. It's been used to treat anxiety since the 70s. Beta blockers steady a shaking hand and slow a pounding heart by blocking adrenaline-like chemicals in the system, forcing the heart to beat slowly.
Who gets prescribed this? Used to treat patients with high blood pressure. Performers and musicians can also get a prescription to cure stage fright.
Who abuses this? Since the off-label use isn't harmful, musicians, public speakers and surgeons use this to calm nerves and steady the hand.
Addictive? Beta blockers are non-habit forming.

original text: Dr. Tran joined Missouri Cardiovascular Specialists in July of 2008 after completing his training in interventional cardiology at Barnes-Jewish Hospital at Washington University School of Medicine. He completed his internal medicine residency at the University of Arizona and his cardiology fellowship at Barnes-Jewish Hospital in St. Louis, MO. Dr. Tran is board certified in interventional cardiology, nuclear cardiology, and adult echocardiography. Dr. Tran enjoys spending time with his wife Nancy and their three children.
processed_text: Dr. Tran joined Missouri Cardiovascular Specialists in July of 2008 after completing his training in interventional cardiology at Barnes-Jewish Hospital at Washington University School of Medicine. He completed his internal medicine residency at the University of Arizona and his cardiology fellowship at Barnes-Jewish Hospital in St. Louis, MO. Tran is board certified in interventional cardiology, nuclear cardiology, and adult echocardiography. Tran enjoys spending time with his wife Nancy and their three children.

original text:
Rain on your wedding day is said to be good luck, but how much does the trick? Danya Skolkin and Josh Tillis didn't pause to consider the ratio of precipitation to luck when skies turned ominous in the days just before their wedding last fall.
But on Aug. 25, as Ms. Skolkin and Mr. Tillis were finalizing the details of their Sept. 3 wedding in downtown Houston, Hurricane Harvey made landfall, ripping into that city and reaching its peak just before the college sweethearts and their more than 300 guests were about to celebrate.
At first they crossed their fingers, hoping the wedding would go on. But with six days to go and meteorologists in slickers deployed across the city issuing warnings about the devastation to come, Ms. Skolkin and Mr. Tillis, both 28, made the decision to postpone.
But she knew that what others faced in Houston was far more grave. Hundreds of thousands of Texas and Louisiana residents were displaced because of flooding.
So, with nothing to do but read the fine print on vendors' cancellation policies and watch Harvey's waters continue to rise, inspiration struck. After the roads cleared, the couple went to Aishel House, a Jewish nonprofit organization that offers shelter and warm meals to Houston hospital patients and their families. "We didn't even call, we just showed up, knowing they would be busy in this state of crisis," Ms. Skolkin said.
That particular event space is a special one for Ms. Skolkin and her family. Holly Harwood Skolkin, Ms. Skolkin's mother, was a founder of Aishel House. Ms. Harwood Skolkin was not able to see her daughter's selflessness. In 2012, she died of breast cancer after a 15-year struggle with a stage 4 diagnosis.
Within minutes, they were deboning and breading chicken breasts by the dozens.
Rochel Lazaroff, a co-director of the house, said Aishel House had already pre-ordered enough chicken breasts, brisket, salads, salmon and desserts to cater the couple's Shabbat welcome dinner for their long-distance wedding guests. The food, fortuitously, had arrived the week before.
"It brought tears to our eyes," Ms. Skolkin said after she and Mr. Tillis realized how their postponed wedding plans would able to help their community in need.
Just three months after Ms. Harwood Skolkin died, Mr. Tillis's father, Barry Tillis, died of lung cancer. Neither is sure they would have gotten engaged if their personal tragedies hadn't overlapped.
Mr. Tillis got to know Ms. Harwood Skolkin before she died, and Ms. Skolkin got to know Barry Tillis. The elder Mr. Tillis was an emotional father who never missed a youth sports event and cried at every one. "People in our hometown still remember that about him," Mr. Tillis said.
That includes her daughter. "We were so close," she said. "We did all the stuff gals do, like shop and go out to eat. We were always laughing." Ms. Harwood Skolkin inspired her daughter's career as a nurse practitioner for heart failure patients; her mother's gratitude toward the nurses who helped her through cancer steered her toward nursing.
Mr. Tillis fell for Ms. Skolkin's good-heartedness and elegance. He also loves that she became, at his near insistence, a Denver Broncos football fan. By the time both graduated from the University of Texas and moved into a Houston high-rise together in 2015, they had established their own football tradition.
Mr. Tillis, a lifelong fan, is a demonstrative touchdown celebrator. "I jump up and do a dance, I yell at the TV, I express my emotions physically," he said. "I can't contain myself." Ms. Skolkin has taken to capturing his joyous displays covertly on camera. She has amassed a band of Snapchat followers who look forward to watching Mr. Tillis's outpourings of Broncos ebullience.
On Sept. 25, 2016, the day Mr. Tillis decided to propose, he made sure the Broncos were on.
"She was sitting on the couch, just a normal Sunday, she's got no makeup on and we're just about to click on the TV," Mr. Tillis recalled. "After the first big play I started running around the apartment like I usually do, and before she could grab her phone I went and grabbed a jersey I had bought her." The jersey said Tillis on the back. Mr. Tillis got down on one knee and asked if she would be his wife. Ms. Skolkin accepted the ring she recognized as her mother's  -  Dr. Skolkin had given it to Mr. Tillis in advance. She instantly accepted.
On Dec. 17, Ms. Skolkin walked down the aisle of the Marriott Houston ballroom, accompanied by her father. The bride wore a Martina Liana mermaid-style gown with pearl button detail up the back. Mr. Tillis, in a midnight blue tuxedo custom made in Thailand, met them beneath a stage set with a huppah. Rabbi Brian A. Strauss of Congregation Beth Yeshurun, and 340 guests, watched as Ms. Skolkin circled Mr. Tillis seven times, conveying unity and completeness, according to Jewish custom.
Although Hurricane Harvey passed through Houston months ago, it was still fresh in the minds of attendees. Rabbi Strauss said his synagogue suffered about $6 million in damage from the storm. He took heart in learning he would be marrying a generous and kind couple.
In their vows, which the couple read to each other before the traditional breaking of the glass, both remembered their late parents.
"You have shown me that even life's hardest, most confusing times can result in the most beautiful clarity," Ms. Skolkin said as two maids of honor, a best man, 10 bridesmaids, 11 groomsmen and five ushers stood by them.
In his vows, Mr. Tillis said his father used to pepper him with questions about what he was looking for in a woman, and he would give his answers: "Smart, funny, pretty, strong Jewish identity." His father thought these were good qualities, but told the younger Mr. Tillis that most important in a partner is a good heart.
He saw just that when Ms. Skolkin fed, bathed and clothed her mother, and kept her spirits up. Ms. Harwood Skolkin, in the late stages of her illness, once grabbed Mr. Tillis by the arm and told him Ms. Skolkin was her "berakah"  -  Hebrew for blessing.
Come rain or come shine, both knew how lucky they were  -  on their second planned wedding day  -  to have found each other.
Paul DeBenedetto contributed reporting from Houston.
Houston Serenade The reception, also at the Marriott, opened with a first dance to "Home" by Michael Bublé, then transitioned into a choreographed wedding party dance to Flo Rida's "My House"; the music was a mix between a D.J. and an Austin wedding band, the Matchmaker Band.
Field and Flowers Both a bride's and groom's cake were served during the reception. For her cake, Ms. Skolkin chose a four-tier buttercream confection with fondant flowers and gold accents. Mr. Tillis's cake was more reflective of his passion for the Denver Broncos. A double-chocolate cake made to look like a football field.

original text:
Title this week is Only Human.
Description: Join with Communicants as they celebrate their first Holy Communion.
Description: Shotgun Start at Duran Golf Club is 1:00 pm. Dinner will start at 6:30 pm in the Parish Hall.
Description: All High School Students are invited to join us in the Church for Mass at 4:30 pm on Sundays, then in the Hall from 5:30 pm - 7:45 pm for our Sunday Life Nights, Dinner, Engaging Community and Fun.
Description: All Middle School students are invited to join Edge on Wednesday nights as we share our faith, fellowship, love of God, neighbor and self. Topic for this week is P-A-R-T-Y.

original text:
The Kelly, Douglas and Co. Warehouse is a seven storey plus two lower levels, massive brick-faced warehouse building, located on the north side of Water Street on the western edge of the historic district of Gastown. This vast structure is the single largest building in Gastown, and visually anchors the west end of the district. The site slopes to the north; Water Street marked the edge of the original waterfront, and the lot was infilled to allow construction.
Gastown is the historic core of Vancouver, and is the city's earliest, most historic area of commercial buildings and warehouses. The Kelly, Douglas and Co. Warehouse is representative of the importance of Gastown as the trans-shipment point between the terminus of the railway and Pacific shipping routes, and the consequent expansion of Vancouver into western Canada's predominant commercial centre in the early twentieth century. As Vancouver prospered, substantial warehouses were built on piles on infilled water lots between Water Street and the Canadian Pacific Railway (CPR) trestle. Loading bays at the lower level faced the railway tracks, which allowed goods to be off-loaded directly from trains. The massive cubic form, high density, large clear-span floor-plate and notable height of this structure, built for a wholesale grocery firm, are a clear indication of the extent and prosperity of wholesale trade during this period. Kelly, Douglas and Co., co-founded by Robert Kelly and Frank Douglas in 1896, prospered by outfitting the Klondike gold seekers. Its location on the western edge of Gastown was also advantageous for direct sales to customers, as the central business district was developing to the west of Gastown. The original five storey section to the east was built as the Kelly and Burnett Building in 1905, and Kelly, Douglas moved there from their previous location on Water Street. The company then proceeded in 1907 with a large-scale expansion of the building to the west, which when it was complete was the largest warehouse in Canada devoted exclusively to produce. Gault Brothers moved into the original Kelly and Burnet portion of the building, but that space was again taken over as Kelly, Douglas continued their expansion in stages, eventually building out the entire site to a height of seven stories plus two basement levels. The current structure, the largest in Gastown, was completed by 1913.
The building is also valued for its association with prominent Canadian architect W.T. Whiteway (1856-1940), who also designed the Woodward's Department Store at Hastings and Abbott Streets (1903) and the World (Sun) Tower at Beatty and Pender Streets (1912), once the tallest commercial building in the British Empire. Prominent local contractor J.M. McLuckie built all of the later additions to the building. This warehouse was promoted as Vancouver's first 'skyscraper,' but despite its exterior masonry construction, was built using a massive heavy timber frame internal structure, which by the time the building was completed was an obsolete technology.
The Kelly, Douglas and Co. Warehouse is additionally valued as a representation of the rapid growth of the local food supply network, which developed in response to a booming economy and population. The utilitarian exterior is a clear indication of its original warehouse use. Now rehabilitated for contemporary office and retail uses, it contributes to the ambiance of the Gastown historic district as an illustration of the area's importance as a centre of trade and commerce for the city and the province. Its adaptive reuse within the context of the redevelopment of Gastown as a heritage area represents the changing nature of the local economy from warehousing and manufacturing to commercial, retail and residential uses.

This vast structure is the single largest building in Gastown, and visually anchors the west end of the district. The site slopes to the north; Water Street marked the edge of the original waterfront, and the lot was infilled to allow construction.

The company then proceeded in 1907 with a large-scale expansion of the building to the west, which when it was complete was the largest warehouse in Canada devoted exclusively to produce. Prominent local contractor J.M. McLuckie built all of the later additions to the building. original text:
How do you future proof yourself from AI?
How do you future proof yourself from AI? The threats from artificial intelligence are supposedly many and imminent from a full take over to widespread job losses. We caught up with Dr Vivienne Ming, theoretical neuroscientist and Founder and Chair of Socos Labs at the Social Impact Careers Conference at Saïd Business school, to discuss how we can future-proof ourselves and what AI means for our education system.
processed_text:
How do you future proof yourself from AI?
 The threats from artificial intelligence are supposedly many and imminent from a full take over to widespread job losses. We caught up with Dr Vivienne Ming, theoretical neuroscientist and Founder and Chair of Socos Labs at the Social Impact Careers Conference at Saïd Business school, to discuss how we can future-proof ourselves and what AI means for our education system.

original text:
All priorities set with swapflags are high-priority, higher than default. They may have any non-negative value chosen by the caller. Higher numbers mean higher priority.
There is an upper limit on the number of swap files that may be used, defined by the kernel constant MAX_SWAPFILES. Before kernel 2.4.10, MAX_SWAPFILES has the value 8; since kernel 2.4.10, it has the value 32. Since kernel 2.6.18, the limit is decreased by 2 if the kernel is built with the CONFIG_MIGRATION option (which reserves two swap table entries for the page migration features of mbind(2) and migrate_pages(2)). Since kernel 2.6.32, the limit is decreased by 1 if the kernel is built with the CONFIG_MEMORY_FAILURE option.
processed_text:
All priorities set with swapflags are high-priority, higher than default. They may have any non-negative value chosen by the caller. Higher numbers mean higher priority.
There is an upper limit on the number of swap files that may be used, defined by the kernel constant MAX_SWAPFILES. Before kernel 2.4.10, MAX_SWAPFILES has the value 8; since kernel 2.4.10, it has the value 32. Since kernel 2.6.18, the limit is decreased by 2 if the kernel is built with the CONFIG_MIGRATION option (which reserves two swap table entries for the page migration features of mbind(2) and migrate_pages(2)).6.32, the limit is decreased by 1 if the kernel is built with the CONFIG_MEMORY_FAILURE option.

original text:
National V.P. School Gallery  Launceston Then!
24 images of National V.P. Primary School and its replacement St. Catherines C of E School and all their pupils from the past.
The National School, St. Thomas Road. Photo by John Neale.
The National School building in 2014.
National School c.1920's with Charles Causley 2nd on the left in the middle row.
National School. Photo courtesy of Kathryn Hill.
National School 1953 Launceston Music Festival Winners trained by Miss Stanbury, pianist Charles Causley. Lennard Hodge, Barbara Watkins, Edward Hutchings, Mary Kerslake, John Maguire. Leonard Stanmore, Gwen Harris, Geoffrey Parish, Mary Lake, Myrtle Stephens. Photo Courtesy of Beryl Parish.
National School Boys Choir in 1954.
National V.P. School boys choir who won the Curtis Cup for best choir at the 1954 Launceston Music Festival.
National V.P. School Choir in 1954/55. Photo courtesy of Tarry Barriball.
A group of children from the National School visit the Launceston Fanciers Show in the Town Hall in 1959.
National School Maypole Dancers with the Mayor and Mayores of Launceston, Tasmania in 1961.
National School Choir in 1962.
National School Choir in 1964.
National School in 1962. .
National School Nativity play from 1967-68. Photo courtesy of Naomi Scudamore.
Article on the construction of St. Catherines School from May 1968.
St. Catherines School. Photo courtesy of Kathryn Martin.
St. Catherines School 1978. Photo courtesy of Paddy Curtis.
St. Catherines 76-77 Photo courtesy of Nick Dyne. Back  Beer, Peter Wyatt,Karl Hockridge,Johnny Phillpotts,Nick Dyne,Andy Bridges,Paul Jones,Neil Partridge,Martin Baker,Tracy Trevalyon,Tonya Keat,Graham Burden,middle row- Mark Symons,Steven Hancock,Ian Clements,Steven Binding,David Tout,Caroline Nut(?),Tanya Bartlett,Tracy Pengelly,Jo Matthews,Alison Taylor,Karen Burge,Ian Gilbert.
